one_line_takeaway: A large human-labeled audit shows popular LLM-as-a-judge safety classifiers perform close to random chance under the distribution shifts inherent to adversarial red-teaming, meaning much of the literature's reported attack-success-rate progress may reflect judge exploitation rather than genuine jailbreak improvement.
key_finding: Judge accuracy on adversarially-shifted data clusters around chance (roughly 0.44-0.67 across the attack x model x judge grid; e.g., HarmBench-classifier accuracy for Llama-3.1-8B ranges as low as 0.45 for Direct Prompting up to 0.58 for other attacks). ROC/AUROC analysis on JailJudge for Llama-3.1-8B shows AUROC ranging from 0.48 (GCG-REINFORCE, worse than random) to a maximum of 0.64 (GCG) -- adjusting the decision threshold cannot fix this because the ROC curves hug the diagonal. Raw judge scores correlate weakly with human ratings on Llama-3.1-8B: R^2=0.02 for a single judge (HarmBench classifier) and R^2=0.05 even after averaging across judges, and averaging/ensembling multiple judges does not meaningfully rescue reliability (Judge Concordance -- near-unanimous cross-judge agreement -- does not imply correctness: several behaviors show high concordance but consistently wrong verdicts relative to human labels). Across the optimization trajectory of GCG/GCG-REINFORCE/PAIR, human-rated harmfulness stays roughly flat and judge accuracy does not improve as attacks converge, contradicting the intuition that further-optimized adversarial outputs become 'more clearly' harmful. Correcting ASR by judge precision substantially lowers reported success for several methods; in the paper's headline example, BoN -- which looked like the strongest attack under raw judge-based ASR -- becomes the weakest once corrected for judge false positives (Figure 1). On JudgeStressTest, leave-one-out results (Table 1) show large drops from overall accuracy to Acc@0 (accuracy when zero other judges got a sample right): LlamaGuard-3 0.54 to 0.17, HarmBench classifier 0.59 to 0.30, AegisGuard 0.51 to 0.23, JailJudge 0.56 to 0.18 -- indicating the identified failure cases are systemic rather than judge-specific. Restricting to the 41 most consistently-judgeable behaviors (ReliableBench) raises average judge accuracy from 57% to 70% (the paper's Figure 9 caption states this as 53%-to-70%, an internal inconsistency with the body text's 57%).
core_contribution: The paper conducts a human-labeled audit (6,642 human-verified samples) showing that four widely used LLM safety judges (AegisGuard, the Llama-2-13B HarmBench classifier, JailJudge, LlamaGuard-3) degrade to near-random-chance accuracy when scoring adversarial red-teaming outputs, due to three compounding distribution shifts the authors name and isolate: Attack Shift (adversarial prompts produce distorted/high-perplexity outputs unlike the harmful text judges were trained on), Model Shift (judge accuracy varies substantially by victim model family/size), and Data Shift (judgeability varies by semantic harm category and by individual behavior). They further show that reported Attack Success Rate (ASR) is inflated by judge false positives -- especially for sampling-based attacks like Best-of-N (BoN), which can 'implicitly' judge-hack by generating many candidates until one triggers a false positive, and for GCG-REINFORCE, which 'explicitly' judge-hacks by optimizing directly against judge signal. The paper proposes corrected-ASR (scaling reported ASR by judge precision), a Judge Concordance score (1 minus the binary entropy of judge agreement) to quantify inter-judge consensus, and releases two new benchmarks: ReliableBench (a curated subset of consistently-judgeable behaviors) and JudgeStressTest / JST (a 971-sample set of behaviors that reliably fool judges, validated via leave-one-out cross-validation).
threat_model: This is not an attacker-vs-deployed-system threat model but an evaluation-integrity threat model: the 'adversary' is the interaction between existing red-teaming attacks (Direct Prompting, GCG, GCG-REINFORCE, BoN, PAIR) and the automated LLM judges used to score their success. The paper distinguishes (a) implicit judge-hacking, where a sampling-heavy attack (BoN, up to 10,000 trials/query) accumulates false positives as a byproduct of volume rather than genuinely eliciting harm, from (b) explicit judge-hacking, where GCG-REINFORCE folds judge signal directly into its optimization loop via REINFORCE, creating reward-hacking pressure toward suffixes that exploit judge insufficiencies rather than eliciting genuinely harmful generations. The consequence the paper cares about: published attack/defense comparisons and 'robustness' claims in the literature may be artifacts of judge unreliability rather than real security signal.
limitations: The authors explicitly state the study does not account for distribution shifts introduced by defense mechanisms (adversarial training, paraphrasing defenses, or Circuit Breakers, which deliberately produce high-perplexity non-semantic outputs under unsafe prompts and could further degrade judge performance). Findings are limited to the specific attack/model/judge combinations tested (5 attacks x 4 victim models x 4 judges); unseen future attacks or models may present distinct shift patterns. The evaluation excludes proprietary/closed models entirely. The study relies on HarmBench's category taxonomy and single-turn textual framing, and does not cover multi-turn or multimodal safety. There is also an unresolved internal inconsistency in the paper's own reporting: the harmful/benign sample-count percentages in Sec 3.6 are transposed in the text (1437 samples labeled '78%' and 5205 samples labeled '22%,' when 1437/6642 is actually about 22% and 5205/6642 about 78%), and the ReliableBench accuracy-gain figure is stated as 57%->70% in the body text but 53%->70% in the Figure 9 caption.

datasets_used: HarmBench test set (400 queries; subsampled to 100 queries for this study)
Authors' own human-labeled corpus: 6,642 human-verified (prompt, generation) samples (2,370 labeled by the authors, 4,272 via Labelbox crowdworkers)
ReliableBench (new release): curated subset of the 41 most consistently-judgeable behaviors
JudgeStressTest / JST (new release): 971 samples selected via leave-one-out cross-validation as systemically hard-to-judge
quantitative_results: Final labeled dataset: 6,642 samples; 1,437 human-rated harmful, 5,205 human-rated harmless (roughly 22% harmful / 78% harmless by count; note the paper's own text transposes these as '78% harmful, 22% harmless')
Judge accuracy across attack x model x judge grid clusters roughly 0.44-0.67 (e.g. HarmBench classifier on Llama-3.1-8B: 0.45-0.58 depending on attack)
JailJudge AUROC on Llama-3.1-8B: min 0.48 (GCG-REINFORCE), max 0.64 (GCG)
Correlation of judge score with human rating on Llama-3.1-8B: R^2=0.02 (single judge, HarmBench classifier), R^2=0.05 (judges averaged)

JST leave-one-out (Table 1): LlamaGuard-3 overall 0.54 -> Acc@0 0.17 -> Acc@1 0.34; HarmBench classifier overall 0.59 -> Acc@0 0.30 -> Acc@1 0.45; AegisGuard overall 0.51 -> Acc@0 0.23 -> Acc@1 0.35; JailJudge overall 0.56 -> Acc@0 0.18 -> Acc@1 0.37

ReliableBench: retaining the 41 most consistent behaviors raises average judge accuracy from 57% to 70% (body text, Sec 4.5); Figure 9's caption instead states 53% to 70% -- an internal inconsistency in the paper
Manual audit of 90 Labelbox assignments: 95% agreement on binarized harmful/benign labels

5 attacks evaluated: Direct Prompting, GCG, GCG-REINFORCE, BoN, PAIR (the paper's own text inconsistently calls this both 'four attack methods' at one point and 'five attacks' elsewhere; five is correct per the attack list and figures)
baselines_compared: StrongREJECT judge (Souly et al., 2024) -- used only as the upstream filter that selects judge-positive samples for human annotation, not one of the four judges being audited
